transcript.text: Hey, Kristina.
How's it going?
This meeting is being recorded.
Definitely been a busy week.
I feel like this is the craziest week of the year.
You know, the holiday rushes a lot, but the first week back is actually maybe more.
How's your experience this week been?
I'm actually off this week, but prioritizing this call because we're really excited and wanting to get it going.
But I'm off this week, so it's been pretty.
It's been fine, but I'm getting a lot of messages.
So I know next week is going to be insane for me.
Well, kudos to you for doing a little bit of work while you're out of office.
That's impressive.
What part of SF are you in?
So I live in Noe Valley.
Do you live in San Francisco?
I live in Houston, but I was in SF for three years.
Okay.
Where did you live?
Yeah, lived in Potrero one year, which was boring.
And then Haight-Ashbury was my closest intersection the second year, which was too much.
I went for like one extreme.
And then did you stay there for the remaining two years that you were in San Francisco or?
No, I move around too much.
The third year I found the sweet spot, North Beach, which who doesn't love North Beach?
I spent most of my time in San Francisco in Cow Hollow and then was in Russian Hill for a bit.
And now I'm in Noe.
So Noe is definitely quieter and probably boring like Potrero, but it's OK.
You know, the person I am now would be looking for boring versus crazy.
It's so funny.
I was in the marina and my facial place is like above what used to be my favorite.
Hey, you're missing this part.
So it's going to sound really random.
So now my facial place is above what used to be my favorite bar.
And it's hilarious because I'm like, this is what I'd rather be doing now than before.
So anyways.
So yes.
It's hilarious how fast that changes.
Right.
um
I remember, like, I used to go to, since I'm from Texas, used to go to Jackson sometimes, the country bar.
That's what I'm talking about, so that's...
No way!
I was going to ask you which bar.
Wow, small world.
Caroline, what part of SF are you in?
I am in NOPA area, which is a good time.
There's lots of families around, which is always fun.
I love it.
It's probably my favorite part of SF.
It's hard to get better than that, especially with the food.
You have way too many restaurants just outside your door.

It's honestly dangerous, and there's a Buy Right, which is, like, my favorite grocery store ever, and so I refuse to ever go anywhere else and spend way too much money on, like, local groceries, but...
I love it.
I love I love it.
Well, great to me, and excited to chat.
Yeah, for sure.
As a quick intro, I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX, and we have a sales team of one, so I work hand-in-hand with Marcel, like, literally...
Every day, every week, we're chatting about something, and I was brought on so that he could focus on all the other parts of the business.
But love the context that you provided.
Definitely think, like, high level, like, we are a partner that does exactly what you outlined.
We'll go into that in detail today, but maybe I could just get quick intros from you both.

And I'm curious how you heard of us, and, you know, I guess if you thought about, like, what kind of content you'd want to create, or if that's something you're looking to the partner for guidance around, anything you think might be relevant for today.
you want to go first, and then I can share more context on, like, what we've done to date and how we're thinking about it.
Yeah, so I'm Kristina.
I've been at Factory less than a month.
I think it's, like, two weeks or two and a half weeks at this point.
But I field in partner marketing and working on other things in marketing as well.
How I heard about GrowthX was through my, I used to report to him, Ryan Francis at Abnormal.
He leads part the marketing department over there.
And he said that he's seen, he was like, I didn't even know it was possible to get these results.
So he said the best things about you guys.
And then also I had been researching Augment Code a while ago and went back and looked at like and did some search and realized like so much had changed.
And when I saw them listed as one of your customers, I was like, wow, you did a really good job because the change is like substantial that I saw with them.
So super cool.
And we also work with Redis now, which I saw you were at before Factory.
So all the companies you worked at.
I wasn't at Augment, but I was, yeah, I have a few friends with her.
So anyways.
Not Augment.
Yeah.
Augment not included.
Yeah, for sure.
Awesome.
And then I'm Caroline.
I lead all things strategy and operations at Factory.
So been here for about a year and a half now, which is a long time in startup world.
My background is in investing.
So I was investing.
for about two and a half years, incubating companies as well, and then made the shift to full-time operating.
And when I started, we were about 15 people, and now we're 44.
So it was around when I was doing everything from our investor relations and board decks, as well as ordering our office snacks.
So have been through everything in building website, building out content.
And we're so lucky to have KZ on now to help us with everything in terms of marketing, partnership marketing, and all that good stuff.
But one thing that we have never really had time for or put energy into is SEO as well as AEO.
And I don't know if you guys do both, but primarily SEO is something that we are heavily underutilizing.
We've, like, ran some, like, like, SEMrush reports and done some work in, like, metadata on our website, which we host ourselves.
We don't use Webflow or anything like that.
But there's a lot more we can do.
We need to build out competitor pages.
need to build out use case pages.
We're writing more customer case studies.
We have no backlinking right now.
There's a ton of open opportunity.

We just haven't had the capacity to actually do it, which is why we're chatting with folks like you to help us figure out what is highest leverage, where should we be putting our time and energy to get the results that we want.
But yeah, I'll pause there.
That's great.
Yeah, I think that's a good starting point.
The only things I want to dig into a tiny bit more is just like who's creating content today.
Is it like before KZ?
Like, was it freelancers?
Is there anyone in-house?
No, it's myself or VP of operations, Francesca.
Or we have some technical blog.
That has done some of the content, no?
Command?
No, they haven't written anything today.
Oh, got it.
Okay.
Yeah, everything today has been written by us.
We have some technical blog posts on our website that some of our engineers have written or CTO, but pretty much anything has been by us.
Okay, awesome.
What type of agency was that that you mentioned?

We work with a PR agency called Command, and they help us with, like, they just, they're helping us now, like, draft some case studies and stuff, but we're still heavily involved in actually writing the content.
Understood.
And then we always, like, dig into the size of the search opportunity, like, before we get into the partnership, just to make sure there's, like, enough for us to make an impact on.

Who would you say, like, the main competitors that you're trying to steal traffic from are, whether they're direct competitors that come up in the sales cycle or companies that are winning in the areas where you want to win in search?
Yeah, I think there's a bunch of different ones.
Like, I would say Cognition.
They have a tool called Devon.
That's definitely one.
I would say Cursor isn't directly competitive, but like if you look at AI software development, they definitely come up a ton.
So we'd like to take some mindshare from them.
Sourcegraph is another one.
I think generally like Cloud Code.
So like obviously Anthropa gets a ton of traffic, but their Cloud Code page specifically.
Anything that is around like AI software development, like we want to be top of mind.
And so I would say like Cloud Code, Codex by OpenAI also is up there.
And then if you want to like loop it into Cognition, Winsurf as well.
Would you also like co-pilot?
Not really.
Not really.
As much.
Super helpful.
And then last question is just like, who are these specific titles that you're targeting?
Like who are your main personas that you want to be on the website that have the highest likelihood to convert?
This is a good question.
So we have two go-to-market motions.
We have an enterprise sales motion where we.
target CTOs, VP of engineering at like Fortune 2000 companies.
But really, we also have a self-service model.
So any developer anywhere we want on our website.
I think from like an enterprise sales, I would say CTO, VP of engineering.
But we really want to be maximizing for any engineer anywhere.
No focus on like backend versus frontend?
No, any engineer.
Yeah.
Why is that?
Just like to help me navigate what you do a little better.
Like any engineer.
So we're a little, I mean, a little bit more about us and like what we've built.
So we've built an agent harness for software development.
But the idea is that we have built an agent that can do anything across the software development life cycle.
So it can do frontend coding tasks.
It can do backend tasks.
It can do infra.
It can work across any language, any type of engineering.
It's also model agnostic.
So you can use any model that you want, any preferred model.
But we made
A tool that wasn't specific to a front-end, back-end, you name it, but is capable of doing anything that a software engineer generally needs to do.
And the idea is that we're trying to roll this out within massive enterprise companies for their entire engineering team.
Like specifying just for front-end engineers would not give us the market opportunity that we're looking for.
Super helpful and interesting.
I think there's a ton of content opportunities just kind of getting the high-level digest from you.
And the Augment Code story is probably like the best example to talk about in terms of like our approach for them, where they wanted to win, what the actual ROI looks like.
So I'll go into that also.
for what it's worth, I think all of the competitors I mentioned are also building for all engineers.
None of them are building for a specific type of engineer.
They also have self-service models in addition to the enterprise sales motion.
So I would say that it's pretty consistent across all of the competitors I named.
Makes sense.
Makes sense.

It's always the best when you can benefit from PLG and enterprise sales, but sometimes the trick is like, how do you do both without over-indexing on one or building, right, like subpar versions of the PLG version and the enterprise version?
So I digress, but it sounds like you guys have a good balance on how you're moving both sides forward.
So the high level on GrowthX, I mean, we are focused, right, on AI search, but it's rooted in SEO best practices.
We see them as complementary.

We know that there's different things that you need to do for both, but to say that, like, being audience first and being the best answers to your audience's questions at scale and looking into specific keywords to target against a difficulty of ranking and competitor content aren't going to move the needle, right, for GEO, like, would be crazy.
They're going to move the needle on both sides.
But the benefit, as you know, is if you can get in AI answers, the people that are...
For us, not the people, we who are looking at ChatGPT and other tools, we see it as expert advice.
You know, we'll ask validating questions, but if we get on a website from a ChatGPT prompt, our likelihood of spending more time coming in more educated and more likely to convert is higher.
And so we are an end-to-end partner.
Strategy, we will come up or piggyback off of work you've done on SEO and GEO and give you a strategy.
We'll create a roadmap of assignments.
So we'll have a six-month content roadmap of all the different content that we could create as we move into the execution phase of the partnership.
And every week, our goal is really to do five articles every single week.
Because with AI search, there is a need to go really deep on a topic, to approach it from like all these different angles and perspectives with the way that someone can go in and prompt.
And so for us, it's really like quality at scale is the differentiator and being.
Being able to get to quality much faster than trying to build AI workflows on your own or hire a team of writers to do that job.
So we'll do all the LLM monitoring and visibility, set up the prompts that you should track, show you how you're improving on a daily, weekly basis.
And we'll also pivot our strategy where the data points us.
And so that's a big thing about what we do is that it's never just rooted in assumptions.
It's always starting with keywords and then looking at content performance to change what we publish and prioritize next.
That was a mouthful, but any questions jumping in your head or things you want me to double click on?
Yeah, would love to hear a little bit more.
So like you mentioned, five articles a week.
Like what are those articles usually comprised of?
So we obviously have our own content that we are releasing that like we want to release at a certain cadence.
We try to at least do like one blog post a week, whether that's a case study, a partnership announcement, you name it.
Would those five be in a.
Addition to what we already have scheduled and then what would comprise those five.
And then I guess I have another question, but I'll hold that.
Yeah, for sure.
So we typically start off with like editorial content, which is typically on your blog, or maybe we stand up a different section of site, like a slash guides.
That's what we did with Augment Code.
And so I'm going to send you some basically like homepages where all the content that you see is created by GrowthX.
That'll give you a good feel for what we would be writing.
But as you look at Augment Code, right, a lot of it is thought leadership in the AI space.
It's listicles or comparisons to different tools.
And as you continue to scroll through here, you'll see that we've published almost like 300 guides for them.
And so that's what's represented in those five.
Typically, the content teams are really excited because instead of having to crank out a bunch of blog articles, you can focus more on case studies or.
Like annual reports and e-books, things of that nature.
That's, I would say, how we work really well in tandem with the team is just creating that bandwidth so that all you're doing in terms of the blogs or the editorials reviewing drafts.
Got it.
And so that makes a ton of sense.
How do we focus?
And I'm curious how your other customers have done this.
Is, like, how do you keep the content quality incredibly high, but then also maintain the trust of your users so it doesn't feel, like, spammy or, like, you're just doing it for SEO?
Like, I see, like, 300 articles and I'm like, there's no way all of those are actually thought leadership.
And, like, that's just my first reaction.
So I'm curious how you, like, work that balance.
Obviously, I know it's important to have all of them for SEO.
Like, could you have some of them hidden?
Like, how do you approach that in keeping the quality and the perception?
That you are only releasing content that is of the highest quality and is legitimately thought leadership.
Yeah, really good question, Caroline.
And like looking at the blog, I was, you know, kind of coming into this call, like, what are they expecting us to create?
Is it like content written by developers, right?
That's like these intensive thought leadership pieces, or is it more SEO content?
And so Augment Code is a good example.
You know, they don't have our guides in the main header of their site.
But the guides are constantly cited in AI Answers.
So they have set up a different section of their site that's in a footer so that it's not losing trust with an audience that, I mean, we still feel like this content is pretty darn good, right?
If you look at some of these pieces, you'll see that it's not just, you know, putting content out for content's sake.
But the result with Augment has been really impressive.
We started in June.
They were around 20.
Monthly Organic.
Now they're
They're over 60, so 3x in six months.
They started spending on paid to diversify the channels.
They've completely shut off the paid spend based on the results that we're driving.
Examples of that, you know, best enterprise AI coding tools.

They're not in front of GitHub in this answer, but one of the articles that we wrote is cited in the bullet point for GitHub, which we think is a great way of, like, incepting into an audience's mind.
They start to see your content show up.
They start to see your name more frequently.
They're more likely to take you seriously.
Look at traditional search.
Enterprise AI coding tools were ranked second there after the sponsored results.
And I think most importantly, by being audience first in the engagement, we really think about, well, how are you differentiated, right?
What is the story that you're trying to tell?
And so for Augment, we've been able to create content that inserts them in the answer around the context window that they have.
you.
you.
Wait.
Which is an advantage.
They can handle like a larger context window, larger code bases.
This is more appealing to enterprises.
You can see that Augment code is listed as the leader.
And there are like a ridiculous amount of citations of content that we've written in this search here.
So that's just like a quick look at the content we've created and like the results is driven for Augment.
Did you also help them with, so when I looked, I don't even know, it was probably like six or seven months ago.
Like when you would search for them, the Reddit threads that were come up were not favorable for them.
And now when you search for it, it's like has the glowing Reddit threads for them.
So I don't know if that was, did you help?
Yeah.
I would have to double check with the team.
I know that we, with clients we've worked with longer, like we experiment with different channels, different work streams.
We can even like add it as a work stream if we want to have full conviction that this is something we're going to do for the next six.
12 months, but we always start off with the marketing site because that's where we're going to drive results the fastest.
Yeah.
was just curious if that was, if you guys worked on that too.
Totally possible.
Not something that's like fully fleshed out to be honest with you.
Yeah.
So to tell you a little more about like how this works, I'll tell you more about Marcel.
I'll kind of go back to that.

But in terms of our partnership, we have this platform that we use internally and we're actually launching an AI visibility product like Profound that you'll have the benefit of being able to log into and use.
But we have this platform our internal team uses where the workflows are housed and the workflows, they go through all the most important factors for AEO.
And I'll show you like how that comes to fruition.
But what's cool about this is that there's a human in the loop for every step.
And so we were very careful about building out our delivery team under the guidance of...

Matthew Panzarino, who was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch for over a decade, and we've got folks who are, like, the SEO experts or even the people that built out the website for places like G2.com that are supporting our clients.
And so there's a great mix.

And the reason I think we've been able to attract that talent is because Marcel is just, I'm not sure if you know much about him, but, like, he's built organic content engines throughout his career, whether it was IBM, where they launched this microsite security intelligence.

That was live for, like, over a decade before coming back in the parent site, their highest ROI marketing channel, to the marketing leader at HashiCorp from six to a hundred million, the CMO at scale through the acquisition by Meta.

And then, like, because he had, like, gone about this process building huge teams and, like, investing a lot into writers, he, at DeepGram, spent a ton of time, like, learning how to use Aerox and different workflow orchestration tools.
And there were...

Results were insane because you could automate the processes that took the most time while keeping the human touch on, you know, the things that you can't use AI for, like the keyword analysis, like figuring out which assignments to prioritize, like being able to write something on brand, right, or that will resonate with an audience.
And so it was this experience that led him to do these workshops.
He's a build like, he's a big build in public kind of guy.
We've got, you know, this community AI led growth that we just started.

And he quit his job at DeepGram basically because people just wanted him to build the content engine for them and it was no longer sustainable, right, to keep doing that job and also try to do some services.
So like, it's cool because our approach, it was services first.
And now we're moving into products and platforms with all this knowledge about like what actually helps you be successful from ideation to content creation to LLM monitoring.
That's like way too much info on us.
But does it help just kind of tell the story of like who we are, how we're different?
I know there's like a lot of agencies out there that say they use AI, but like this is coming from a bit of a different positioning in place.
Definitely.
Very helpful.
I did have, just going back a bit, I had a question around, you had mentioned that you have writers that write a lot of this content for us.
So two questions around that.
One, what does the involvement from our team look like and how involved would we be in writing that content?
And then two, what, like, what is the background of those writers?
Like, are they technical writers?
Do they, like, I'm just curious on what their background is and their understanding around the CodeGen space.
Yeah, definitely.
So I'm sure you see like a lot of logos that are more technical here.
You mentioned Abnormal, talk about Augment Code, Airbyte, Redis, list goes on.
But we have a good mix.
We have technical writers.
We have folks who are more SEO experts and write.
About more general business topics.
And the relationship is that all you have to do is review our drafts.
We want to calibrate in the first part of the engagement.
So we do this strategy sprint.
It's an eight-week accelerated engagement.
You can opt out if you're not feeling confident before the growth execution and the 18K.
We can definitely talk about that.
I know that's like sticker shock usually without much context behind it.
But we would calibrate with you in a kickoff call and a product deep dive.
We would ingest resources from your team.
And then we would build these artifacts, which we would want you to help us confirm, right?
Like represent your brand voice, your personas, your products and services.
And then we start creating calibration articles where you can help us hit the quality bar.
But that's really the involvement that we need.
From there, it's, you know, we're working on these five assignments.
We're showing you the data.
We're sending the drafts.
And then we're even staging and publishing in your CMS.
So that, you know, you can can...
can...
Basically have your time back to do whatever other marketing initiatives or content initiatives will be most helpful.
I think something that would be helpful is like we, so right now we are very hands-on, right?
All of our content internally.
And I think to do this at the scale that we want for it to return on our investment, like it seems like in this phase one, we would need to be super hands-on.
Is that, is that the case?
I wouldn't say super hands-on, more so like quick to give us feedback on the artifacts that we create.

So the artifacts, just jumping into like augment codes, we have artifacts that really try to capture the most important details around the type of engineering manager that they're going after, the language that they use.
Yeah.
Put this together and then we give you feedback on the drafts that you do.
Yeah, I would say in the first two weeks, you know, like we want to have an hour kickoff.
want to have a 45 minute product deep dive.
We have 45 product deep we
We want to potentially do a follow-up to capture different nuances since it's more technical.
But after that, it's really just like, here's the artifacts.
Here's the calibration articles.
Give us feedback on how to improve this.
Like what's the period before we actually get going on content?
Yeah, for sure.
So we have this, it says work in progress, but it's pretty legit, I would say.
So we have this process where like, we're sending the artifacts in week one or two.
We're developing calibration articles by week four.
And then we're working on actual articles that we would publish week five and onwards.
The only thing that gets in the way of this timeline is just not getting a quick review and feedback from the team.
Yeah, I think just candidly, like we are hoping to start getting content out ASAP.
And so like the eight weeks feels like a long time, obviously in startup world.
And it's like, we have a ton of launches coming up.
We are trying to change this like tomorrow.
Um, so wanted to see, like, do we need the strategy sprint?
Are there ways to expedite the strategy sprint?
How are there ways that we, we can expedite this in any shape, way, or form so that we can start getting content ready sooner?
Um, and I think candidly, like, would also want to see two to three samples of, like, the type of content that you would create for us before even engaging in a strategy sprint.
Yeah, for sure.
I hear you.
Um, so I guess I'll say that the phase one, our goal is to move towards publishing as fast as possible.
So the faster that we can calibrate, the faster that we can agree on the roadmap of assignments we want to execute on, we'll move to publishing at that point.
So it's not just strategy.
You could expect, for some clients, we get 20 articles published live on their website by the end of that eight weeks.
In terms of samples, the best that I could do is just share you examples of the clients that we've worked with that are more technical.
do How are together.
and See
We've got like a waitlist typically of kickoff interest and we just don't have a formal sort of like POC process and also let you opt out from phase two.
So that is the other piece that is kind of something you can take away.
Awesome.
Helpful.
I know we got three minutes left and I do have to jump to another call.

I would love to show you like a proper demo and I guess since you want to move fast, if there's anybody that's going to be involved in the decision making process, maybe we could bring them along as well.
What would make the most sense for you both?

Yeah, I think what would be super helpful is if you could send us that like the augment page that you guys have really owned and like any maybe the deck that you shared, like any data that we can bring to our team.
And then from there, we can loop in Christina, maybe Francesca and schedule a demo.
But I think we first want to show them kind of like what the.
Results are and what it would look like before scheduling next steps.
Yeah, no problem.
I will send you all the relevant examples, the deck, the recording, a few recordings from Marcel that just talks about like what we do and the problem we're solving.
And then I'll also include some client success stories so that you can actually point to results.
But I'm glad we made the intro here and KZ, don't work too hard while you're at office, okay?
I appreciate it.
Awesome.
Thanks, Tyler.
Anything else, KZ?
No, I think that covers it all for now.
And I'll take a look at all the everything you sent through.
And yeah, cool.
Awesome.
Awesome.
Excited to keep the conversation going.
Appreciate your time.
Yeah, I really appreciate it.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[GrowthX pitches its AI content service to Factory for SEO growth.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=189.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Factory's Goal:** Rapidly build SEO/AEO authority for its AI agent harness, which targets all engineers (frontend, backend, infra) and has both enterprise (CTOs, VPEs) and self-service GTM motions.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=323.0)
  - [**GrowthX's Pitch:** An end-to-end service delivering 5 articles/week to build deep topical authority, using a human-in-the-loop AI platform to ensure quality and scale.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=682.0)
  - [**Key Friction Point:** Factory wants to publish content immediately, but GrowthX's process includes an 8-week, $18k "Strategy Sprint" for calibration before full-scale execution.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1543.0)
  - [**Path Forward:** Tyler will send Augment Code case studies and content examples for Factory to review internally before scheduling a full demo with key decision-makers.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)

## Topics

### Factory's SEO Challenge & Goals

  - [**Need:** Build SEO/AEO authority from a near-zero baseline.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=332.0)
  - [**Current State:**](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=332.0)
      - [Limited SEO work (metadata, SEMrush reports).](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=332.0)
      - [No backlinking strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=363.0)
      - [Content created in-house by Caroline (Head of Strategy) and Francesca (VP of Ops).](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
  - [**Competitors to Target:**](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=460.0)
      - [Cognition (Devon)](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=460.0)
      - [Cursor](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=460.0)
      - [Sourcegraph](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=460.0)
      - [Cloud Code (Anthropic)](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=460.0)
      - [Codex (OpenAI)](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=460.0)
      - [Winsurf](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=460.0)

### GrowthX's Proposed Solution

  - [**Service:** End-to-end content partnership for AI search (AEO) and traditional SEO.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=682.0)
  - [**Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=738.0)
      - [**Strategy:** Develop a 6-month content roadmap based on keyword analysis.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=738.0)
      - [**Execution:** Deliver 5 articles/week to build deep topical authority.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=759.0)
      - [**Monitoring:** Track LLM citations and content performance to pivot strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=788.0)
  - [**Methodology:** A human-in-the-loop AI platform, guided by a team with experience from TechCrunch and G2.com.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1189.0)
  - [**Case Study (Augment Code):**](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=627.0)
      - [**Results:** 3x organic traffic in 6 months (20k → 60k/mo), allowing them to pause all paid spend.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1011.0)
      - [**Strategy:** Published \~300 guides in a separate `/guides` section to build authority without diluting the main blog's thought leadership.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=982.0)
      - [**Impact:** Content is cited in AI answers and ranks highly in traditional search, helping to frame the market around Augment's strengths (e.g., large context window).](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1030.0)

### Partnership Model & Timeline

  - [**Phase 1: Strategy Sprint ($18k, 8 weeks)**](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1389.0)
      - [**Purpose:** Calibrate on brand voice, personas, and product details.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1406.0)
      - [**Process:** Kickoff, product deep dive, artifact review, and calibration articles.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1493.0)
      - [**Outcome:** A content roadmap and, for some clients, 20+ live articles.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1589.0)
      - [**Opt-Out:** Factory can exit after the sprint if not confident in the partnership.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1389.0)
  - [**Phase 2: Growth Execution (Ongoing)**](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1428.0)
      - [**Cadence:** 5 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=759.0)
      - [**Workflow:** GrowthX handles writing, staging, and publishing; Factory provides draft feedback.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1334.0)
  - [**Factory's Concern:** The 8-week sprint is too long; Factory wants to publish content immediately.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1543.0)
  - [**GrowthX's Response:** The timeline can be expedited with quick feedback. A formal POC is not offered, but the sprint's opt-out clause serves as a trial.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1543.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Tyler:** Send Factory the following:](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)
      - [Augment Code case study and content examples.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)
      - [GrowthX pitch deck and Marcel's recordings.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)
      - [Additional client success stories.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)
  - [**Factory (Kristina, Caroline):** Review materials internally with Christina and Francesca.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)
  - [**Factory:** Schedule a full demo with key decision-makers after the internal review.](https://fathom.video/share/GJeZTfoLbXkMpcFdcwpkLNwAg1FaEPdv?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)

action_items.description: Email Caroline/Kristina Augment Code guides, deck, recording, Marcel videos, client success stories; then they review/share w/ Francesca
